summary:
Trichloroacetic acid is preferred for local application in managing molar hypersensitive dentin due to its potent causticity enabling targeted tissue modification with reduced collateral damage versus alternatives such as alcohol, chlorhexidine, or silver nitrate which lack this level of precision while still aiming at similar therapeutic goals indirectly through other mechanisms unrelated specifically to desensitizing dental surfaces efficiently. \boxed{TCA}
The summary highlights how TCA provides a specific benefit by being both powerful enough to treat localized problems related to hypersensitive dentin and minimally invasive when applied correctly, distinguishing itself from alternative treatments based primarily on indirect effects or lower efficacy levels tailored differently than needed here clinically speaking regarding direct intervention into tooth surface sensibility reduction tasks within oral health care settings involving posterior teeth regions prone frequently experiencing discomfort symptoms associated medically termed 'dentin hyperplasia'. 

Key Points Summarized In Two Sentences For Clarity And Conciseness While Maintaining Core Clinical Relevance To Question At Hand Regarding Most Effective Caustic Treatment Option Available Today Within Modern Dentistry Practice Guidelines When Dealing With Molars Afflicted By Hypersensitivity Issues Specifically :

* Among options considered including alcohols ,chlorhexidines etc.,trichloroaceticacid emerges superior .* 
Therefore,\boxed{\text{{T}}}\_c\_a.\]
